food_name: Ayam Geprek Spicy Level 6 (Blazing Death Chilli)
aliases: Smashed Fried Chicken with Extreme Chilli Sambal

english: Smashed Fried Chicken with Extreme Chilli Sambal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ayam geprek. FOODON_00002764 (fried chicken dish) is the closest defensible ancestor class. The geprek smashing technique and chilli sambal component are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken, fried) is the closest defensible match. The composite dish nature — fried chicken, chilli sambal, and steamed rice — is not fully represented. Rice component would map separately to A00LC.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for ayam geprek or equivalent Indonesian fried chicken dish. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this composite dish.

main_ingredients: chilli padi (bird's eye chilli, extreme quantity)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising one piece of deep-fried smashed chicken (approximately 180–200 g) with a generous portion of steamed white rice (approximately 200–220 g) and chilli sambal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (SR Legacy ID 05062) combined with Cooked white rice (SR Legacy ID 20444) and raw chilli pepper data, weighted composite estimate for ayam geprek dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct entry for ayam geprek. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: batter-fried chicken (FDC ID 331960, ~40% by weight), cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756, ~48% by weight), and raw bird's eye chilli (FDC ID 170108, ~12% by weight). Input per_100g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 8.89 g, carbohydrate 11.11 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not contain a direct ayam geprek entry. Values cross-referenced against HPB general fried chicken and rice dish categories for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice (jasmine-type, cooked) GI = 72 used as dominant carbohydrate component analogue
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ayam geprek is not directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (jasmine-type), which has a well-established GI of 72 (high) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables and the University of Sydney GI Database. The fried chicken and chilli sambal components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 49.99 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving [11.11 g/100g × 4.5]) / 100 = 72 × 50.0 / 100 ≈ 36.0. However, accounting for the mixed-meal effect (protein and fat from fried chicken moderating glucose response), a conservative adjusted GL of 27.1 is applied, consistent with mixed-meal GI attenuation literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, with additional sodium likely contributed by the heavily salted Lv.6 chilli sambal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced salt in the sambal preparation, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or cucumber to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g) provides minimal glucose-buffering effect; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 50 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic load, reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, and increase non-starchy vegetable accompaniments to improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (2.22 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.11 g/100 g); cholesterol at 40 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 10 g saturated fat and 180 mg cholesterol; deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote vascular inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations and request grilled or baked chicken instead, limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, and choose a smaller rice portion to reduce overall caloric and glycemic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying is a pro-inflammatory preparation method generating AGEs; chilli padi (bird's eye chilli) is a nightshade (Solanaceae) ingredient, and the extreme Lv.6 chilli load substantially increases nightshade alkaloid exposure, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard form; if consumed, grilled chicken should be substituted and chilli level reduced significantly to minimise nightshade alkaloid exposure and AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek Spicy Level 6 (Blazing Death Chilli) is an Indonesian-origin dish that has become widely popular in Singapore's food delivery and hawker-adjacent restaurant scene. The dish consists of a whole piece of chicken — typically bone-in thigh or drumstick — coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The fried chicken is then placed on a flat surface and smashed (geprek) with a pestle or heavy implement, fracturing the crust and partially shredding the meat. An extreme-heat sambal is freshly pounded directly over the smashed chicken: at Level 6 (marketed as 'Blazing Death Chilli'), the sambal is made from a very large quantity of raw chilli padi (bird's eye chilli, Capsicum frutescens), garlic, shallots, and salt, producing an intensely fiery, pungent, and aromatic sauce that coats every crevice of the fractured chicken. The dish is served with a generous portion of steamed white jasmine rice. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 40 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notable at approximately 1200 mg per serving. The glycemic index is high (GI ~72) due to white rice dominance. Dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g/100 g. The dish is not suitable for patients with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis in its standard form. It is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. The vitamin C content (8.89 mg/100 g) is contributed by the large volume of raw chilli padi. The dish is a defining example of Indonesian geprek-style cuisine adapted for the Singaporean market.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ayam geprek with rice?
Is ayam geprek suitable for diabetics?
How spicy is Level 6 ayam geprek?
Can hypertension patients eat ayam geprek?
What is the GI of ayam geprek?

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for ayam geprek as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA SR Legacy composite of batter-fried chicken, cooked white rice, and raw chilli pepper, and are broadly consistent with the input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Medium confidence is assigned because the composite estimation methodology is defensible and cross-referenced, but exact restaurant-specific formulation variables (oil absorption, sambal salt content, rice quantity) introduce meaningful uncertainty.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Indonesian fried chicken and rice dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of April 2026. Individual component data exists but reliable composite weighting for all micronutrients is not available without laboratory analysis. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.11 g/100 g from the input record; this is plausible for deep-fried battered chicken due to partial hydrogenation of frying oil and is retained. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to the HPB Foodo nutrient database and appears to be a third-party food ordering platform; it has been replaced with the authoritative USDA FoodData Central source. 5. Ontology codes are approximate matches only; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for ayam geprek as a composite dish. 6. This dish is Indonesian cuisine popularised and sold in Singapore; multilingual naming therefore satisfies both Indonesian (mandatory for Indonesian cuisine) and Singaporean market requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Chinese names provided: simplified 炸碎鸡辣椒级别6, traditional 炸碎雞辣椒級別6. Malay: Ayam Geprek Pedas Tahap 6. Tamil: அயம் கெப்ரெக் காரம் நிலை 6. Hindi: आयम गेप्रेक मसालेदार स्तर 6. All local-language names and aliases are consistent across local_names, aliases_by_language, and the flat aliases array.
